quizzes.title: W09 Quiz 1
quizzes.description: Exodus 19 as a seam-filled Sinai chapter with conditional covenant language, priestly identity tension, and rising mediation

questions.question: How does the lecture characterize Exodus 19:5?

options.text: As an unconditional royal promise
As a conditional covenant statement before detailed stipulations are named
As a summary of the Holiness Code
As a rejection of covenant language

questions.explanation: The lecture emphasizes that Exodus 19:5 is conditional—'if you listen/obey'—even though the detailed legal stipulations have not yet been laid out.

questions.question: Which repeated wording at the opening of Exodus 19 is treated as a clue to emphasis or compilation?

options.text: 'Moses went up'
'The mountain smoked'
'They encamped there'
'The people trembled'

questions.explanation: The opening repetition of 'they encamped there' is highlighted as either rhetorical emphasis or evidence of compiled material.

questions.question: What tension does the lecture see in the phrase 'kingdom of priests'?

options.text: It conflicts with the Exodus chronology
It suggests all Israel is priestly, yet later priests are distinguished from the people
It denies Moses’ leadership
It removes holiness language from Sinai

questions.explanation: The lecture notes tension between all Israel as a 'kingdom of priests' and later references that distinguish priests from the people.

questions.question: Why is Exodus 19:9 called theologically striking in the lecture?

options.text: Because it denies God’s holiness
Because it says the people will never hear God
Because God comes so the people will hear and believe in Moses forever
Because it replaces Moses with Aaron

questions.explanation: The purpose clause in 19:9 elevates Moses’ authority: God comes in the cloud so the people may hear and believe Moses forever.

questions.question: Which odd instruction in Exodus 19:15 is singled out as unexpected in the chapter?

options.text: Build an altar of uncut stones
Do not go near your wives
Offer incense on the mountain
Choose seventy elders

questions.explanation: The lecture flags the instruction not to go near wives as an unexpected addition not clearly grounded in a prior command in the chapter.

questions.question: What narrative seam is noted between Exodus 19:25 and 20:1?

options.text: Moses dies before the Ten Commandments
Aaron speaks instead of God
19:25 ends as if Moses will speak, but 20:1 continues with 'God spoke'
The people ascend the mountain with Moses

questions.explanation: The transition from 19:25 to 20:1 is treated as a seam suggesting rearrangement or layered composition.

quizzes.title: W09 Quiz 2
quizzes.description: Exodus 19–24 as a narrative of increasing distance, the people’s request for Moses as mediator, and the framing of the legal corpus

questions.question: What main trajectory does the lecture trace from Exodus 19 through Exodus 24?

options.text: Israel gains direct and easy access to Yahweh
Distance between Yahweh and Israel increases, requiring mediation
The law replaces the covenant
The tabernacle eliminates all danger immediately

questions.explanation: The lecture argues that Exodus 19–24 is arranged to show increasing distance between Yahweh and Israel and the growing necessity of mediation.

questions.question: How does Exodus 20:18–21 function in the lecture’s reading?

options.text: As a contradiction of Exodus 19
As a resumptive repetition of Sinai theophany imagery that highlights fear and distance
As the formal close of Leviticus
As a song of Moses

questions.explanation: Exodus 20:18–21 reprises the Sinai imagery to reemphasize the people's fear and their distance from God.

questions.question: What request do the people make in Exodus 20:19 according to the lecture?

options.text: That Aaron become king
That God stop giving laws
That Moses speak to them instead of God, lest they die
That they may all ascend the mountain

questions.explanation: The people ask for Moses to mediate the words because direct divine speech terrifies them.

questions.question: Which passages are said to bracket the legal corpus in Exodus 20:22–23:33?

options.text: Exodus 19:1 and 25:1
Exodus 20:22 and Exodus 24:1
Exodus 18:1 and 24:12
Exodus 21:1 and Leviticus 1:1

questions.explanation: The lecture highlights a narrative and grammatical linkage between Exodus 20:22 and Exodus 24:1 as a frame around the laws.

questions.question: In Exodus 24, who alone draws near to Yahweh?

options.text: All Israel
Aaron and the seventy elders
Nadab and Abihu
Moses

questions.explanation: Though others are included in layers of access, only Moses actually draws near, reinforcing mediation.

questions.question: What problem does the tabernacle address, yet not fully solve, in the lecture?

options.text: A shortage of priests
The distance and presence problem of Yahweh dwelling among Israel
The lack of food in the wilderness
The absence of kingship

questions.explanation: The tabernacle is presented as an answer to the distance problem, but the golden calf and later restrictions show the problem persists.

quizzes.title: W09 Quiz 3
quizzes.description: Why God gave the law, how law functions in the Pentateuch’s literary structure, and why Moses’ mediation remains necessary

questions.question: What two guiding questions organize the lecture’s treatment of Exodus 19–Numbers 10 and beyond?

options.text: Who wrote the Pentateuch and when was it canonized
Why God gave the law and how law functions in the Pentateuch’s narrative structure
Why Israel wanted a king and how the temple was built
How the prophets used Genesis and why wisdom literature matters

questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly frames the unit around why God gave the law and what role the law plays in the Pentateuch’s narrative design.

questions.question: What contrast does the lecture make between reading the Pentateuch as 'window' and as 'structure'?

options.text: Window means fiction, structure means history
Window emphasizes historical events, structure emphasizes crafted literary-theological design
Window means law, structure means gospel
Window focuses on Greek, structure on Hebrew

questions.explanation: The lecture contrasts reading the text as a historical window with reading it as a literary-theological structure shaped to communicate meaning.

questions.question: How does the lecture treat common Christian categories like law/gospel or civil-ceremonial-moral divisions?

options.text: As the Pentateuch’s own organizing logic
As irrelevant to Christian theology
As noted frameworks, but not the Pentateuch’s native organizing categories
As commands explicitly given in Exodus 19

questions.explanation: These frameworks are acknowledged, but the lecture says they are not the Pentateuch’s own internal organizational scheme.

questions.question: What major narrative-theological role of the law is emphasized?

options.text: To abolish mediation
To highlight Moses as mediator and expose Israel’s need for a mediator
To replace covenant promises with ritual
To teach Israel military strategy

questions.explanation: A central function of the law in the narrative is to elevate Moses’ mediatorial role and reveal the people's need for mediation.

questions.question: How does the lecture describe the law’s effect within the Pentateuchal narrative?

options.text: It fully transforms Israel’s obedience
It is useless and ungracious
It is gracious and wise, yet limited in its power to sanctify Israel
It matters only for priests

questions.explanation: The law is presented as gracious and wise for Israel’s historical setting, but the narrative shows its limited power to change the heart.

questions.question: Which later Numbers sequence is highlighted near the end of the lecture’s overview?

options.text: Deborah’s song and Gideon’s fleece
Balaam’s poems and Israel’s idolatry with Baal in Numbers 25
The rebuilding of the temple
The anointing of Saul

questions.explanation: The lecture points to Balaam’s oracles in Numbers 23–24 and Israel’s Baal-related idolatry in Numbers 25 as part of the continuing narrative arc.
